one_line_takeaway: A 2025 survey tracing NIDS from signature-based to NN-based to LLM-integrated systems, whose real value is its taxonomy and its balanced conclusion that LLMs should augment - not replace - traditional NIDS while also being a dual-use offensive threat.
key_finding: Signature-based detection (Snort, Suricata, Zeek) remains the practical mainstream because of transparency, stability, and low false-positive rates on known threats, while NN-based detection - despite two decades of MLP/CNN/RNN/LSTM/AE/DBN/Transformer variants - still stalls in deployment due to labeled-data scarcity, class imbalance, poor robustness (distribution shift and adversarial examples), and non-interpretability. LLMs add contextual reasoning, adaptability, and explainability, but the survey concludes they cannot yet replace purpose-built real-time NIDS because of data-representation mismatch (network flows/payloads are not natural language), high compute/latency cost, adversarial and prompt-injection vulnerability, hallucination/overconfidence, and privacy risks. The authors' recommended posture is a hybrid stack: lightweight rule/anomaly engines for front-line real-time filtering with LLMs used as augmenting modules for context, labeling, and interpretation, adapted via CPT+SFT and kept fresh with RAG and PEFT updates.
core_contribution: A narrative survey by Feng and Sakurai (Kyushu University; arXiv:2510.23313v2, Nov 2025, cs.CR) that systematizes the evolution of Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) across three eras: signature-based, neural-network (NN)-based, and LLM-integrated. The authors' OWN contribution is the organizing framework, not any new experiment: an organizational map (Fig. 1) and four summary/taxonomy tables (Table I comparing signature tools; Table II categorizing NN-based approaches; Table III categorizing LLM-based NIDS approaches; Table IV categorizing LLM offensive-capability studies), a per-era synthesis of strengths and limitations, a practitioner-oriented guide to domain-adapting general LLMs to NIDS (continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, PEFT/LoRA, prompt engineering), a catalog of seven concrete mitigation strategies for LLM-in-NIDS deployment (Sec. IV.E), and a taxonomy of how LLMs are weaponized offensively (Sec. V). The four headline takeaways: (1) signature-based NIDS remain foundational despite known weaknesses; (2) NN-based detection, though studied for 20+ years, still is not reliably deployable in practice; (3) LLMs are useful but face serious practical challenges and can be exploited offensively; (4) NIDS-specific LLMs should be adapted from pretrained models rather than trained from scratch.
threat_model: Dual, and genuinely present in the paper (not forced). Defensive framing: NIDS detecting network intrusions (zero-day attacks, evasion/obfuscation, multi-stage attack chains, IoT/IoV/CAN and cloud environments). Offensive framing (Sec. V): adversaries exploiting LLMs across the attack lifecycle - reconnaissance, automated penetration testing (e.g., PentestGPT, HackSynth, VulnBot, RapidPen as surveyed), malware and attack-traffic generation, phishing/social-engineering, and deliberate generation of benign-looking, NIDS-evading traffic (e.g., AdvTG, Incalmo as surveyed). The survey also stresses adversarial-robustness and prompt-injection/jailbreak risks that arise when an LLM is itself placed inside the detection loop, plus privacy/data-leakage concerns from feeding logs and payloads into LLMs.
limitations: As a review it is broad but shallow per method, with a mostly qualitative treatment and no synthesized quantitative comparison across surveyed systems. It does not disclose a systematic search/inclusion methodology, so coverage completeness cannot be assessed. It reports no experiments of its own, so the review contributes no new empirical evidence. Minor internal inconsistencies exist (e.g., Zeek is given as version 8.0.3 in prose but 8.0.4 in Table I; reference [135] is listed under both 'Conventional Networks' and 'IoV Networks' rows of Table III). Some surveyed claims are relayed without critical appraisal of their evaluation quality.

datasets_used: None used by the authors - this is a survey with no experiments. Datasets referenced as used by SURVEYED works include: KDD99/DARPA, NSL-KDD, CIC-NIDS2018 (CIC-IDS2018), CICIoT2023, and TON-IoT.
quantitative_results: The survey itself reports NO quantitative results of its own (no experiments).
Cited from surveyed work [206] AdvTG: over 40% attack success rate (ASR) across six detection models on six datasets.
Cited from surveyed work [204] Incalmo: an LLM using the Incalmo abstraction layer successfully executed multi-stage attacks in 9 of 10 network environments; a smaller LLM succeeded in 5 of 10.
Cited from surveyed work [175] PentestGPT: evaluated on a penetration-testing benchmark of 13 scenarios and 182 subtasks, reporting higher task-completion than a baseline LLM.
Reference-scale figures cited for context: Mistral 7B has 7 billion parameters; LLaMA-4 is stated as ~2 trillion parameters.
baselines_compared: None - the authors run no experiments and therefore compare no baselines of their own. Comparisons in the paper are qualitative (signature-based vs. NN-based vs. LLM-based eras) summarized in Tables I-IV.
